name: rpy2 (R from Python Interface)

performance.notes: R execution speed; data conversion overhead between R and Python

agent_readiness.notes: Run R from Python. import rpy2.robjects as ro. r = ro.r. r['base'].sum(ro.IntVector([1, 2, 3])). ggplot2: from rpy2.robjects.packages import importr; ggplot2 = importr('ggplot2'). from rpy2.robjects import pandas2ri; pandas2ri.activate(). r_df = pandas2ri.py2rpy(pandas_df). py_df = pandas2ri.rpy2py(r_dataframe). Inline R: from rpy2.interactive import process_revents. Magic: %load_ext rpy2.ipython; %%R. DESeq2: from rpy2.robjects.packages import importr; deseq2 = importr('DESeq2'). Requires R installed.

gotchas: Requires R installation with matching bitness (64-bit)
R error messages in Python context can be cryptic
Data type conversion (especially factors, NA) is error-prone
Thread safety issues with multiple R environments
best_when: Using R-only packages (DESeq2, limma, edgeR) from Python workflows
Jupyter notebooks needing R cells alongside Python %%R magic
When migrating R codebases to Python incrementally
Generating ggplot2 visualizations from Python data
avoid_when: When Python equivalents exist (pydeseq2 for DESeq2, lifelines for survival)
Production workflows where R dependency adds fragility
Real-time applications where conversion overhead matters
summary: rpy2 enables calling R from Python. ro.r['stats'].lm(). pandas2ri for DataFrame conversion. importr('ggplot2'). %%R magic in Jupyter. GPL-2.0+ by Laurent Gautier. Best when R-only packages (DESeq2, limma) are needed in Python workflows. Requires R installation. Data conversion is error-prone (factors, NA). Use Python-native alternatives when available.
